g.data-narration: First-Order Logic extends propositional logic with variables and quantifiers for richer representation.
Propositional logic only handles true or false facts. FOL adds variables, predicates, and quantifiers.
g.text: Propositional Logic only handles true or false facts. FOL adds
variables, predicates, and quantifiers to express general rules.
Example: All humans are mortal - forall x (Human(x) implies Mortal(x))

g.data-narration: The five building blocks of FOL are constants, variables, predicates, functions, and quantifiers.
Constants represent specific objects like John or Paris. Variables can take different values like x or y.
g.text: Constants represent specific objects: John, Paris, Socrates, x1, A
Variables represent unspecified objects: x, y, z, person, thing
Terms: constants, variables, or function applications

g.data-narration: Predicates represent properties like Mortal(x) or relationships like Likes(x, y).
Functions map tuples of objects to a single object, like FatherOf(x) mapping to a person.
g.text: Functions map objects to objects: FatherOf(x) maps to John, MotherOf(John) maps to Mary
Terms: constants, variables, or function applications like FatherOf(Mary)

g.data-narration: Universal quantifier means for all x, P(x) holds. Existential means there exists an x where P(x) holds.

g.text: For all x, P(x) is true
There exists x such that P(x)

g.data-narration: Atomic formulas are predicates applied to terms. Well-formed formulas combine atoms with connectives.
g.text: Atoms: Predicate applied to terms - Mortal(John), Likes(x, y)
WFFs: Built from atoms using quantifiers and connectives

g.data-narration: An interpretation assigns meaning to symbols with a domain of objects and a mapping to values.
g.text: An interpretation I = (D, I0) assigns meaning to symbols

g.data-narration: Variables inside a quantifier scope are bound. Variables outside are free and can be renamed.
g.text: Quantifier Scope and Free vs Bound Variables
In forall x (P(x) implies Q(x)), variables inside are bound. Variables outside are free.
forall x [ P(x) implies exists y R(x,y) ] - x and y are bound

g.data-narration: Negating forall x gives exists x not. Negating exists x gives forall x not.

g.text: neg forall x P(x) equiv exists x neg P(x)
neg exists x P(x) equiv forall x neg P(x)

g.data-narration: Facts are specific statements like Human(Socrates). Rules are general statements with variables.
g.text: Facts: Simple statements about specific objects

g.data-narration: Four types: All S are P, Some S are P, No S are P, Some S are not P.
Substitution replaces variables with terms. Unification finds substitutions making two atoms identical.
g.text: Unification: Find substitution making two atoms identical

g.data-narration: Occurs check prevents unifying a variable with a term containing that variable, avoiding infinite structures.
g.text: Unification fails when variable appears in term containing itself:

g.data-narration: Key inference rules include universal instantiation, existential instantiation, and generalized modus ponens.

g.text: forall x P(x) maps to P(c)
exists x P(x) maps to P(c)
P(c) and forall x(P maps to Q) maps to Q(c)

g.data-narration: Resolution is refutation-complete - prove conclusions by deriving a contradiction.
g.text: Resolution is refutation-complete: prove by contradiction
1. Negate conclusion 2. Convert to clause form 3. Resolve to empty clause

g.data-narration: Common mistakes include incorrect quantifier distribution and forgetting the occurs check during unification.
g.text: 1. forall x (P(x) and Q(x)) is not (forall x P(x)) and (forall x Q(x))
2. forall x exists y P(x,y) is not exists y forall x P(x,y) - order matters
3. Always apply occurs check during unification

g.data-narration: FOL extends propositional logic with variables, predicates, and quantifiers for powerful reasoning.
g.text: FOL extends propositional logic with variables, predicates, and quantifiers
forall x P(x) means for all x, exists x P(x) means there exists x
